Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'm 28 and working part-time, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could—usually late at night or during breaks. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often download materials and work through problems physically rather than just reading on screen. Some weeks I'd put in a solid 28 hours, other weeks barely anything. It really depended on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely not regular. Deadlines would hit and suddenly I'd be cramming, but otherwise I'd drift. Work schedules changed weekly, so I couldn't really establish a consistent study time. I'd sometimes go days without touching course material, then binge-watch lectures when I realized something was due. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I survived.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was so spotty. The peer support program actually helped remind me to check in more often. But honestly, if nothing urgent was happening, I could go a while without logging in.
Q4: There was a brief period where logging in became more routine, actually. After I connected with a peer through the support program, we'd check in with each other, which made me more accountable. I started logging in more frequently just to see what was happening in discussions or if my peer had messaged me. It felt less isolating when someone else was in it with me, even if I wasn't doing all the work.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful for me—I watched a lot of them, probably close to 300 minutes total. Being able to pause and rewind helped since I learn by doing and needed to process things at my own pace. I'd often watch a video, then try to apply what I learned hands-on with my own notes. The visual demonstrations stuck with me way more than reading text ever did.
Q6: Discussion boards, definitely. I think I only posted once the entire course. I just didn't see the point—felt like busywork when I was already struggling to keep up with the essentials. Reading assignments I'd often skim or skip entirely. When you're low on resources and time, you prioritize what directly affects your grade, and discussions didn't feel like they moved the needle.
Q7: If I could see how an activity directly connected to passing an assignment or exam, I'd do it. Practical application was key for me—I needed to feel like I was building toward something tangible. Activities that felt abstract or theoretical lost me quickly. When I could physically work through a problem and see results, that felt worth my time.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. I was in survival mode most of the semester, so anything labeled 'optional' got immediately deprioritized. Required activities at least had consequences attached, which motivated me even if I procrastinated. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping up with the mandatory work.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I'd download PDFs and videos, then study them on my phone during bus rides to work or during breaks. I had notebooks full of handwritten notes because writing things out helps me remember better than typing. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely. The LMS was more like a content delivery system than where I actually studied.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was actively studying from printed materials and handwritten notes, but my LMS activity would show almost nothing. I'd watch a lecture once online, download the slides, then spend hours working through problems on paper. My exam score of nearly 68 didn't come from nowhere—I was learning, just not in ways the system could track.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and never go back to the LMS version. Storage on my phone was precious but course materials took priority. I'd review those downloaded files multiple times offline. The LMS would only show one access, but I might have studied that material five or six times.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. Work picked up extra shifts, and I just couldn't make it to classes—my attendance dropped to like 35%. I felt disconnected from what was happening in the course. Surprisingly, I wasn't super stressed about it, but I definitely disengaged. The peer support helped pull me back somewhat, but there were weeks where I just... drifted.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor, honestly. Working part-time with limited resources meant the LMS often took a backseat to immediate survival needs. When I was exhausted after a shift, logging in to watch lectures felt impossible. Confusion with some material made me avoid it rather than engage—easier to just hope it wouldn't be on the exam.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have the LMS open but I was just going through the motions. Scrolling through content without really absorbing anything. My mind would be on work stress or bills or whatever else. The login would register, maybe some video minutes would count, but I wasn't really there mentally. It was more about feeling like I was doing something than actually learning.
Q15: Assignments were my weakness—I only completed about 18% of them, which is embarrassing considering I scored really high on the ones I did submit. I'd procrastinate because the work felt overwhelming, or I'd convince myself I'd do it later and then later never came. The ones I avoided were usually ones requiring discussion or group work, which felt harder to jump into when you've been absent.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see something was due in 48 hours and suddenly I'd be all over the LMS, watching videos, downloading materials, trying to cram. Without deadlines, I'd float. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the main driver of my engagement. My response time averaged over 7 hours, which shows I wasn't exactly proactive.
Q17: When I actually submitted work and got feedback, it did motivate me—especially seeing those high assignment scores. It was surprising and made me think maybe I could actually do this. But feedback came too late sometimes, after I'd already mentally checked out of that topic. The peer support feedback was more immediate and kept me somewhat accountable.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace given my schedule. Bad because without rigid structure, I'd drift and lose track of where I should be. Having 11 online courses meant a lot of self-direction was expected, and I wasn't always great at providing that for myself.
Q19: The video-heavy content worked well for my learning style—I could watch, pause, practice. But the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match how I learn. The sheer volume of content across 11 courses was overwhelming. Clearer weekly checklists might have helped me stay on track instead of constantly feeling lost about priorities.
Q20: That makes total sense to me. You might be logging in, watching videos, exploring content—genuinely trying to understand—but then life happens and you can't complete the actual submission. Or you're anxious about submitting imperfect work, so you prepare extensively but never pull the trigger. The activity shows effort; the missing submissions show life getting in the way.
Q21: That's literally me. My attendance and assignment completion were terrible, but I still passed with a decent exam score. I was learning offline, studying downloaded materials, working through problems by hand. The LMS didn't capture any of that. Some of us just don't learn well in the system but can still demonstrate knowledge when it counts.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long videos play. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the thinking and practicing that doesn't involve a screen. They can't measure understanding or effort, just clicks. A student could have the LMS open while sleeping, or be deeply engaged with printed notes—the log wouldn't know the difference.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to pass. It's when you're curious, when you want to figure things out rather than just check boxes. For me, engagement happened most when I was hands-on with problems, even if that was offline. It's a mental state, not a login count.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I studied from printed notes on the bus, or how my peer support buddy's encouragement kept me from dropping out entirely. It wouldn't show that I'm a 28-year-old working student with limited resources doing my best to balance everything. My high assignment scores when I actually submitted show capability the low completion rate doesn't capture. The full picture is so much more complicated than any log could show.
